artifacts.content:
Hi Mom,

I just wanted to put our Saturday plan in writing so we’re on the same page before things get crazy again this fall.

Starting in March 2024, can we stick with what we talked about on the phone – you watching Ellie and Caleb at your place on 5th Avenue West in Hendersonville every Saturday morning while I run the DuPont “Waterfalls & Forest Light – DuPont Explorer” tours? My goal is to base most of those out of the Hooker Falls Access Area since it’s only about a 35‑minute drive from you. I’d drop the kids off around 8:30 a.m., head straight to Hooker Falls to meet clients at 9:00, and then swing back by your house to pick them up around 2:00–2:15 p.m. once the 4–5 hour tour block wraps up.

It honestly makes the difference between these tours being viable or not. Paying for weekend childcare on top of gas and insurance would eat most of the profit, especially with how slow winters have been. I’m trying to be smart about growing Blue Ridge Adventure Guides LLC without burning out again like I did last spring.

I’ll pack them lunches and snacks so you and Dad don’t have to worry about feeding two bottomless pits. If your arthritis is acting up or you’ve got a doctor’s appointment, just give me a heads‑up the week before and I’ll adjust the schedule.

Thank you again for being willing to do this. The kids love their Saturdays at Nana and Granddad’s, and it lets me keep doing the work I love without feeling like I’m shortchanging them.

Love,
Melissa

metadata.subject: Saturdays this spring – DuPont tours & kid drop-off

artifacts.content:
Hey Patrick,

I just finished blocking out the October 2024 calendar for DuPont, and I wanted to run the Friday plan by you.

I’m going to stack most of the fall‑foliage “DuPont Explorer” outings at the Lake Imaging Access Area on Fridays. If I start those tours right at 9:00 a.m. and keep them to about 4–4.5 hours, I can be back in Asheville by 1:30 p.m. with the car. That way you’re not stranded before your standing 11:00 a.m. telehealth therapy sessions at the house for the driving anxiety and panic attacks.

I know you technically don’t *have* to go anywhere during the session, but you’ve said just knowing you’d be stuck without the Outback if something went sideways makes the panic worse. This way, worst case, you know the car is in the driveway before your therapist logs on, and I’m not trying to race down I‑26 from DuPont watching the clock.

I’ll also avoid booking anything on Fridays that starts from Hooker Falls or High Falls, since the drive time is a bit less predictable. Lake Imaging is just simpler for those days. If a client absolutely needs a different schedule, I’ll push them to a Thursday or weekend.

Let me know if this lines up with what you and your therapist have talked about. I’m proud of you for sticking with the weekly sessions, even when they dredge stuff up.

Love you,
M

metadata.subject: October Fridays – Lake Imaging + your telehealth slot

artifacts.content:
Hey Erin,

I’m so excited you’re coming in from Raleigh that third weekend in October. I’ve been trying to puzzle out how to make it both a family celebration and not a total financial hit, and I think I’ve got a plan.

On Saturday, October 19, 2024, I booked a small “Waterfalls & Forest Light – DuPont Explorer” group starting from the High Falls Access Area at 8:00 a.m. The idea is to take the paying clients on the usual loop and then fold you and Ellie into the Hooker Falls and Triple Falls section as we head that way mid‑morning. We promised her a special hike to celebrate her finishing that occupational therapy block at Mission Children’s Hospital for her sensory processing stuff, and she has been talking nonstop about “showing Aunt Erin the waterfalls.”

Honestly, combining the family hike with the tour is the only way this feels doable right now. Those $85 per‑session copays we’ve been paying since July have added up fast. Being able to offset some of that with a paid outing that same day takes a little pressure off without canceling on Ellie’s celebration.

We’ll probably be wrapped up by early afternoon, then we can grab late lunch/early dinner in Brevard or head back to Asheville. If you can get here Friday night, that would make the early start less painful.

Let me know what time your drive from Raleigh will put you here, and I’ll text you the trailhead pin.

Love,
Mel

metadata.subject: Oct 19 plan – DuPont tour + Ellie’s OT celebration

artifacts.content:
Weekday DuPont scheduling rules – DO NOT BREAK

1. Absolute latest departure from forest: 2:30 p.m.
   - This applies to all “Waterfalls & Forest Light – DuPont Explorer” hikes leaving from Hooker Falls Access Area, High Falls Access Area, and Lake Imaging Access Area on school days.
   - Build in a 30–40 minute buffer for the drive back to Arden so I can be in the Glen Arden Elementary car line by 3:00 p.m. for Ellie and Caleb.

2. Remember February 2023.
   - CPS showing up at 214 Cascade Ridge Lane after that report that the kids were left at school too long was terrifying and humiliating.
   - Even though it was a miscommunication and the caseworker closed it quickly, I do not ever want to risk being late to pickup again.

3. Practical steps:
   - No weekday tours starting later than 9:30 a.m.
   - If weather, traffic, or a client issue threatens the 2:30 exit time, cut the route short (skip High Falls, take the most direct trail back).
   - Pre‑write an explanation email for clients about why weekday hikes end earlier (school pickup, safety, boundaries) and stick to it.

4. Emotional reminder:
   - Protecting my guide reputation is important, but protecting my kids and our stability is more important.
   - I started Blue Ridge Adventure Guides LLC to have flexibility, not to recreate the same guilt and burnout I had before.

Tape this note above the desk and in the glove box so I see it when I’m tempted to squeeze “just one more viewpoint” into a weekday hike.

metadata.title: Weekday DuPont boundaries – car line & CPS scare

artifacts.content:
Tour design brainstorm – Lake Imaging mindfulness segment

Goal: Add a 20–30 minute grounding/mindfulness practice at the end of the Lake Imaging version of the “Waterfalls & Forest Light – DuPont Explorer” hike.

Inspiration: The stress‑management group I did at Asheville Women’s Counseling Center last spring (April–May 2024), when I was totally burned out from trying to grow Blue Ridge Adventure Guides too fast (back‑to‑back weekends, no days off, constant social media). The facilitator’s breathing and body‑scan exercises helped more than I expected.

Outline:
- Find a quieter spot off the main flow near Lake Imaging but still on established trail/cleared area.
- Invite guests to spread out, sit or stand.
- 5 minutes: simple box breathing (explain without calling it “therapy”).
- 10–15 minutes: guided observation (“notice light on the water, sound of wind, etc.”), tie in to photography – they can take a few intentional shots after.
- 5–10 minutes: silent time, then regroup and start hike out.

Boundaries:
- Keep it low‑key; no need to share my own burnout story or mention the Asheville Women’s Counseling Center group.
- Emphasize that it’s optional and people can just rest and drink water if they prefer.

Logistics:
- Build this into the advertised time window so I’m not tempted to skip it when running late.
- Note for myself: this is as much for my nervous system as for clients – a reset before the drive back to Asheville and the evening kid chaos.

Hey Erin,

Catching my breath for a second while the kids do homework, so I figured I’d update you on how I’m juggling all the moving pieces this fall.

First, Saturdays: starting in March 2024, Mom has been a total lifesaver. I drop Ellie and Caleb at their place on 5th Avenue West in Hendersonville around 8:30 a.m., then head to the Hooker Falls Access Area for the “Waterfalls & Forest Light – DuPont Explorer” tours. The 4–5 hour block fits perfectly so I can swing back by around 2:00 p.m., grab the kids, and still get home to Asheville before I collapse.

Weekdays are trickier. After that CPS scare in February 2023 when someone reported the kids being at Glen Arden Elementary too long and the caseworker showed up at 214 Cascade Ridge Lane, I made a hard rule: no weekday DuPont hikes that keep me in the forest past 2:30 p.m. Whether I’m starting from Hooker Falls, High Falls, or Lake Imaging, I build the route backwards from the 3:00 p.m. car line. If clients want a longer day, they get a weekend slot or nothing.

On top of that, I’m trying to be more intentional about my own sanity. Those grounding and breathing exercises from the April–May 2024 stress group at Asheville Women’s Counseling Center have actually stuck. I’ve started sneaking a 20–25 minute “quiet forest light” segment into some of the Lake Imaging outings – officially it’s for guests, unofficially it keeps me from feeling like I’m sprinting all the time.

It’s a lot of plates to keep spinning, but for the first time it feels like there’s a rhythm instead of pure chaos.

Love,
Mel

metadata.subject: How I’m keeping the wheels on this fall

artifacts.content:
Event: Friday DuPont – Lake Imaging foliage tour + Patrick car/therapy window

Date: Friday, October 11, 2024
Time: 9:00 a.m. – 1:30 p.m.
Location: Lake Imaging Access Area, DuPont State Recreational Forest
Attendees: 8 clients + Melissa (guide)

Notes:
- Meet clients at 8:15 a.m. at the usual coffee shop off I‑26, caravan to Lake Imaging.
- Keep route moderate and on established trails; aim to be back at the parking area no later than 1:10 p.m. to allow drive back to Asheville.
- HARD STOP at 1:30 p.m. so I’m home with the Subaru before Patrick’s 11:00 a.m. telehealth therapy session runs over. His weekly online appointment at the house is for the anxiety/panic attacks about driving mountain roads, and he feels safer knowing the car is available if he needs it.
- Build in a 20–30 minute mindfulness/grounding segment near a quiet spot off the main flow by Lake Imaging, modeled on the breathing/observation exercises from the April–May 2024 stress‑management group at Asheville Women’s Counseling Center.
- Double‑check that this doesn’t bump against school pickup: should still have a buffer before heading to Glen Arden if anything shifts.

Reminders:
- Pack extra trekking poles, first‑aid kit, printed DuPont map.
- Leave snack/lunch instructions for kids and confirm with Mom if she’s on backup duty that afternoon.

metadata.title: DuPont Lake Imaging – foliage tour (Patrick telehealth/grounding segment)
